Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a study routine. I'd maybe glance at materials when I felt like it, but there was no set schedule or anything. Most weeks I'd put in maybe a couple hours here and there, usually late at night when I couldn't sleep. I know I should have been more organized, but the whole thing just felt tedious to me.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place, really. Sometimes I wouldn't touch anything for days, then I'd cram when something caught my attention or when I felt a rare burst of interest. Deadlines barely moved me since I wasn't completing most assignments anyway. It was more about my mood than any external schedule.
Q3: Usually boredom, ironically enough. I'd be scrolling through my phone, get tired of social media, and think maybe I should check what's happening in the course. Sometimes the gamified tasks they introduced would pull me in because they felt less like work. Occasionally a notification would remind me the platform existed.
Q4: Not really a routine, but the gamified elements made me check in more often than I would have otherwise. Getting points or seeing progress bars filled gave me something to do when I was bored. It wasn't about learning for me—it was more like a low-stakes game to pass time. That's probably the closest it got to being habitual.
Q5: The videos were decent because I'm a visual learner and I could watch them while doing other stuff. The gamified tasks were engaging in a superficial way—they made things feel less boring. Discussion boards were okay too because I could see what other people thought without having to dig through dense readings myself.
Q6: I basically ignored most of the readings and the detailed assignment instructions. They felt like walls of text that I couldn't bring myself to care about. I'd skim them at best, but usually I just didn't bother. If it wasn't visual or interactive, it probably got skipped.
Q7: If it was quick, visual, or had some kind of reward attached to it, I'd do it. The gamified stuff worked on me even though I knew it was designed to manipulate engagement. Anything that felt like a grind or required sustained focus just made me tune out immediately. I needed instant feedback or I'd lose interest.
Q8: Required activities felt like obligations I resented, so I mostly avoided them. Optional stuff felt lower pressure, which paradoxically made me more willing to try it sometimes. But honestly, the distinction didn't matter much to me—I engaged with whatever seemed least boring in the moment, regardless of whether it was required.
Q9: Yeah, I'd download videos and PDFs and then watch or read them on my tablet away from the computer. Sometimes I'd look stuff up on YouTube if the course explanations were putting me to sleep. I had good resources at home, so I didn't need to stay logged into the LMS to access information.
Q10: When I actually studied for exams, I did it with downloaded materials and my own notes, completely offline. I'd also have conversations with classmates over text about concepts, which obviously doesn't show up anywhere. The LMS would show me as inactive during some of my most productive study sessions.
Q11: Definitely. I'd download a video once, then rewatch it multiple times offline when I was actually trying to understand something before an exam. Same with lecture slides—I'd grab them and then study from my tablet. One download could represent hours of actual engagement that the system never saw.
Q12: The middle of the semester was rough. Everything felt monotonous and I just couldn't make myself care about the material. There was nothing wrong exactly—no crisis or anything—I was just profoundly bored and unmotivated. The stress was there but it wasn't enough to push me into action.
Q13: Fatigue was a big one. I'd be tired and the thought of logging in and doing coursework felt exhausting. Competing responsibilities weren't really the issue since I don't do extracurriculars—it was more internal resistance. When I was stressed, I'd avoid the LMS entirely rather than face what I was behind on.
Q14: All the time. I'd scroll through discussion posts without really reading them, or have a video playing while I was actually on my phone. I logged a lot of activity that was basically just me being physically present but mentally checked out. The system recorded engagement that wasn't really happening.
Q15: The assignments, obviously. I completed barely any of them even though I knew they mattered for my grade. It wasn't that I couldn't do them—when I actually tried on exams, I did well. I just couldn't summon the motivation to care about routine assignments. They felt pointless and tedious.
Q16: Deadlines mostly made me feel guilty rather than productive. I'd see them approaching and think about engaging, but then I'd just... not. Occasionally a deadline would create enough pressure that I'd do something, but usually I'd let them pass and deal with the consequences. It's not a great system, I know.
Q17: When I actually got feedback, it was usually too late to matter because I'd already mentally moved on. Positive feedback on exams reinforced that I could do well without engaging with the regular coursework. Negative feedback on the rare assignment I submitted just made me less likely to submit the next one.
Q18: The structure felt rigid and uninspiring, which didn't help my motivation issues. The gamified elements were the one thing that worked for me because they broke up the monotony. But overall, the course felt like it was designed for students who already wanted to engage, which wasn't me.
Q19: The gamified tasks made engagement easier—they gave me small wins and made logging in feel less pointless. But the heavy reliance on written assignments made things harder because that's where my motivation completely died. More interactive or visual elements would have helped someone like me.
Q20: That's basically me. I logged in a lot, watched videos, participated in discussions, did the gamified stuff—but I barely submitted any actual assignments. For me, it was about engaging with what felt interesting while avoiding what felt like work. Activity doesn't equal productivity.
Q21: That's also me, weirdly enough. Despite my terrible assignment completion, I scored really well on exams because I can absorb information when I actually try. I studied in ways that didn't show up in the LMS, and when it mattered—like exam time—I could perform. Low activity doesn't mean low capability.
Q22: Logs can show patterns of when people log in and what they click on, but they can't measure actual understanding or attention. They miss offline study completely and can't tell if someone's genuinely engaged or just going through the motions. They're measuring presence, not learning.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and wanting to understand it. For me, I was rarely truly engaged—I was just present sometimes. Real engagement would mean I'm curious, motivated, and actively thinking about the material. That's not something a login can capture.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show that I'm actually capable when I try, or that my disengagement isn't about ability but motivation. It wouldn't capture my offline study before exams or the fact that I learn well visually but shut down with text-heavy content. It sees a drifter, not the reasons behind the drifting.
